name: South of Broad
h1: Personal Injury Lawyer Serving South of Broad, Charleston
meta_title: South of Broad Personal Injury Lawyer | Charleston | Roden Law
meta_description: Injured in South of Broad, Charleston? Roden Law handles car accidents, pedestrian injuries, and carriage tour incidents in this historic district. Free consultation.
roads: Meeting Street south of Broad carries a constant stream of carriage tours, tour buses, pedestrians, and vehicles through narrow lanes flanked by historic buildings with zero setback. East Bay Street along the waterfront sees conflicts between high-speed vehicle traffic and pedestrians crossing to the Battery and White Point Garden. Tradd Street and other narrow east-west residential streets force vehicles into tight passing situations with cyclists and pedestrians sharing the roadway. The intersection of Broad and Meeting Streets is one of the most congested and accident-prone junctions in downtown Charleston.
hospitals:
MUSC Health — 171 Ashley Ave, Charleston, SC 29425 — (843) 792-2300
Roper Hospital — 316 Calhoun St, Charleston, SC 29401 — (843) 724-2000
landmarks: The Battery and White Point Garden, Rainbow Row, St. Michael's Church, Four Corners of Law (Broad & Meeting), Charleston Harbor waterfront, Edmondston-Alston House
service_area: Serving South of Broad, the Battery, Rainbow Row, and the historic Charleston peninsula.
content_hook:
South of Broad is Charleston's most iconic and exclusive residential neighborhood, where centuries-old homes line narrow streets that were never designed for modern traffic volumes. The area's immense popularity with tourists creates a volatile mix of carriage tours, tour buses, pedestrians, cyclists, and residential traffic in extremely tight quarters.
Roden Law's office at 127 King Street is located just blocks from South of Broad. Our personal injury attorneys handle cases involving car accidents on Meeting and East Bay Streets, pedestrian collisions with vehicles and carriage tours, bicycle crashes on narrow residential streets, and slip-and-fall injuries on the area's historic brick and stone sidewalks.
Injured in South of Broad? Contact Roden Law for a free case review.

faqs.question: Can I sue a carriage tour company if their horse or carriage injures me?
faqs.answer: Yes. Carriage tour companies have a duty to maintain safe operations and control their animals. If a carriage or horse causes injury due to operator negligence, equipment failure, or an out-of-control animal, the company may be liable. South Carolina law applies strict liability in some animal-related injury cases.

faqs.question: Who is liable for a trip-and-fall on an uneven sidewalk in South of Broad?
faqs.answer: In Charleston's historic district, sidewalk maintenance responsibility may fall on the City of Charleston, the adjacent property owner, or both, depending on the location. Historic brick and stone surfaces are particularly prone to creating trip hazards. An attorney can determine the liable party.

faqs.question: Are pedestrian accidents common in South of Broad?
faqs.answer: Yes. The combination of narrow streets, heavy tourist foot traffic, carriage tours, and vehicles creates frequent vehicle-pedestrian conflicts. The Battery and White Point Garden area, Rainbow Row, and the Broad/Meeting intersection are particular hotspots.

name: French Quarter
h1: Personal Injury Lawyer Serving the French Quarter, Charleston
meta_title: French Quarter Personal Injury Lawyer | Charleston | Roden Law
meta_description: Injured in Charleston's French Quarter? Roden Law handles pedestrian accidents, bar-related injuries, and car crashes in the entertainment district. Free consultation.
roads: Church Street through the French Quarter carries mixed traffic of vehicles, horse-drawn carriages, pedestrians, and cyclists through one of downtown's narrowest corridors. State Street and its intersection with Church Street see frequent pedestrian-vehicle conflicts from gallery visitors and diners. Market Street generates heavy foot traffic from the historic Charleston City Market that spills into the roadway, especially on weekends. East Bay Street along the French Quarter boundary carries high-speed vehicle traffic immediately adjacent to dense pedestrian activity.
hospitals:
MUSC Health — 171 Ashley Ave, Charleston, SC 29425 — (843) 792-2300
Roper Hospital — 316 Calhoun St, Charleston, SC 29401 — (843) 724-2000
landmarks: Charleston City Market, Dock Street Theatre, Waterfront Park and Pineapple Fountain, French Huguenot Church, Vendue Range gallery district, Charleston Harbor
service_area: Serving the French Quarter, Market Street, Waterfront Park, and the historic Charleston arts district.
content_hook:
Charleston's French Quarter is the city's arts and cultural district, bounded by East Bay, Broad, Meeting, and Market Streets. Home to the famous Charleston City Market, Waterfront Park, Dock Street Theatre, and dozens of galleries, the French Quarter draws enormous foot traffic through narrow historic streets shared with vehicles and carriage tours.
Roden Law's office at 127 King Street is steps from the French Quarter. Our personal injury attorneys represent residents and visitors injured in pedestrian accidents, car crashes on Church and East Bay Streets, bicycle collisions, and premises liability incidents at restaurants, galleries, and hotels in the district.
Injured in the French Quarter? Contact Roden Law for a free consultation.

faqs.question: Can I sue a bar or restaurant if an overserved patron injures me in the French Quarter?
faqs.answer: South Carolina's dram shop laws allow claims against establishments that serve alcohol to visibly intoxicated patrons who then cause injury to others. If a bar in the French Quarter overserved the person who injured you, both the individual and the establishment may be liable.

faqs.question: What if I'm injured at the Charleston City Market?
faqs.answer: The City of Charleston and market vendors have a duty to maintain safe conditions. If you're injured due to wet floors, falling merchandise, uneven surfaces, or other hazards, the responsible party may be liable under premises liability law. Document the hazard and your injuries immediately.

faqs.question: How do I find witnesses after an accident in the French Quarter?
faqs.answer: In a busy tourist district, witnesses may be visitors who are difficult to locate later. Get names and phone numbers at the scene, check nearby businesses for surveillance cameras, and contact an attorney immediately. Roden Law can act quickly to preserve evidence and locate witnesses before they leave the area.

name: King Street District
h1: Personal Injury Lawyer Serving the King Street District, Charleston
meta_title: King Street District Injury Lawyer | Charleston | Roden Law
meta_description: Injured on King Street, Charleston? Roden Law handles pedestrian accidents, car crashes, and premises injuries along Charleston's main shopping corridor. Free consultation.
roads: Upper King Street's restaurant and bar district generates heavy nightlife-related pedestrian traffic and elevated DUI risk, especially Thursday through Saturday nights. Lower King Street's retail corridor sees constant pedestrian crossings between shops, with jaywalking common due to widely spaced crosswalks. The King and Calhoun Streets intersection is one of downtown Charleston's busiest and most accident-prone junctions, with CARTA bus traffic, vehicles, pedestrians, and cyclists converging. The King Street and Spring Street intersection marks the transition between residential and commercial zones where speed differentials cause rear-end collisions.
hospitals:
MUSC Health — 171 Ashley Ave, Charleston, SC 29425 — (843) 792-2300
Roper Hospital — 316 Calhoun St, Charleston, SC 29401 — (843) 724-2000
landmarks: Upper King Street restaurant row, Lower King Street antique district, Marion Square, King Street retail corridor, Charleston Visitor Center (nearby), College of Charleston (adjacent)
service_area: Serving the King Street corridor from Broad Street to the Crosstown, and the surrounding downtown Charleston blocks.
content_hook:
King Street is Charleston's main commercial artery, stretching from the historic district near Broad Street through the retail and antique district to the vibrant Upper King restaurant and nightlife scene. Roden Law's own office sits at 127 King Street, giving our attorneys firsthand familiarity with this corridor's daily traffic challenges.
Our attorneys handle personal injury cases along the entire King Street corridor, including pedestrian accidents near retail shops, DUI collisions in the Upper King nightlife district, bicycle crashes, and slip-and-fall injuries at restaurants and commercial properties. We understand the unique patterns of this street — from daytime shopping traffic to late-night entertainment crowds.
Injured on King Street? Walk into our office or call for a free case review.

faqs.question: Are DUI accidents more common on Upper King Street?
faqs.answer: Yes. Upper King Street's concentration of bars and restaurants generates elevated rates of impaired driving, particularly on weekend evenings. If you're hit by a drunk driver, you can pursue a personal injury claim against the driver and potentially a dram shop claim against the establishment that overserved them.

faqs.question: Can I file a claim if I trip on a broken sidewalk on King Street?
faqs.answer: Yes. The City of Charleston or the adjacent property owner may be liable for maintaining safe sidewalk conditions. King Street's heavy foot traffic and aging infrastructure make trip-and-fall hazards common. Document the defect with photos and contact an attorney promptly.

faqs.question: What if I'm injured by a CARTA bus on King Street?
faqs.answer: Claims against public transit involve the South Carolina Tort Claims Act, which has specific notice requirements and deadlines shorter than standard personal injury claims. Contact an attorney immediately to ensure you meet all procedural requirements for your claim against the transit authority.

meta_description: Injured in Harleston Village, Charleston? Roden Law handles car accidents, pedestrian injuries near MUSC, and more. Free consultation. No fees unless we win.
roads: Calhoun Street through Harleston Village carries heavy traffic between downtown and the Medical District, creating dangerous conditions for pedestrians crossing to MUSC and Roper Hospital. Bee Street and the surrounding MUSC campus streets see constant ambulance, shuttle, and patient drop-off traffic that creates unpredictable stopping and turning hazards. Ashley Avenue through the residential core generates cyclist-vehicle conflicts on a designated bike route with no protected lane. The Calhoun and Ashley Avenue intersection near Colonial Lake is a complex junction with poor sight lines and heavy pedestrian volume.
hospitals:
MUSC Health — 171 Ashley Ave, Charleston, SC 29425 — (843) 792-2300
Roper Hospital — 316 Calhoun St, Charleston, SC 29401 — (843) 724-2000
landmarks: Colonial Lake, MUSC Medical Center campus, Roper Hospital, College of Charleston (adjacent), Halsey Institute of Contemporary Art, Harleston Village residential district

content_hook:
Harleston Village is a residential neighborhood on Charleston's peninsula anchored by Colonial Lake and bordered by the MUSC Medical District. The neighborhood's proximity to two major hospitals creates a unique traffic environment where emergency vehicles, medical staff commuters, patient transport, and residential traffic all converge.
Roden Law's Charleston office handles personal injury claims for Harleston Village residents and medical district workers, including car accidents on Calhoun Street, pedestrian collisions near MUSC and Roper Hospital, and bicycle crashes on Ashley Avenue. Our attorneys understand the complex traffic patterns generated by the medical campus and can identify all liable parties in your case.
Injured in Harleston Village? Contact Roden Law for a free case review.

faqs.question: What if I'm hit by a car while walking to MUSC?
faqs.answer: Pedestrians injured by negligent drivers near the medical district have the right to pursue full compensation. The heavy pedestrian traffic around MUSC and Roper Hospital means drivers have a heightened duty of care. Document the scene and contact an attorney before giving statements to insurance companies.

faqs.question: Can I sue MUSC if I'm injured on their campus?
faqs.answer: MUSC is a state institution, so claims follow the South Carolina Tort Claims Act with specific notice requirements and damage caps. However, if a private contractor, vendor, or individual caused your injury on campus, standard personal injury rules may apply. An attorney can determine the best approach.

faqs.question: Are bicycle accidents common in Harleston Village?
faqs.answer: Yes. Ashley Avenue is a designated bike route but lacks protected bike lanes, forcing cyclists to share the road with vehicle traffic. The neighborhood's proximity to the College of Charleston and MUSC generates heavy cycling activity in streets not fully designed for bike safety.

Hasell Street through the neighborhood serves as a major east-west connector, generating cut-through traffic at speeds inappropriate for the narrow residential grid. Anson Street and George Street are popular hotel and B&B corridors where guest pedestrians unfamiliar with the area's traffic patterns are vulnerable. The East Bay and Market Street intersection channels heavy tourist traffic from the Market area into high-speed vehicle traffic.

Ansonborough is a historic residential and boutique hotel neighborhood in downtown Charleston, stretching from East Bay Street to King Street between Market and Calhoun. The district's concentration of hotels, inns, and vacation rentals brings a steady flow of visitor pedestrians who are often unfamiliar with Charleston's narrow streets and fast-moving traffic on East Bay.
Roden Law's Charleston office, located steps away on King Street, handles personal injury claims in Ansonborough including car accidents on East Bay Street, pedestrian collisions involving hotel guests, and premises liability incidents at hotels and commercial properties. Our attorneys understand the liability obligations of hospitality businesses and property owners in the historic district.
Injured in Ansonborough? Contact Roden Law for a free consultation.

faqs.question: Is a hotel liable if I'm injured on their property in Ansonborough?
faqs.answer: Hotels have a heightened duty of care toward their guests under South Carolina law. If unsafe conditions — such as wet floors, broken stairs, inadequate lighting, or security failures — caused your injury, the hotel may be liable for your damages.

faqs.question: What if I'm hit by a car while crossing East Bay Street?
faqs.answer: East Bay Street's high traffic speeds make pedestrian accidents particularly dangerous. Drivers must yield to pedestrians in crosswalks, and the government entity responsible for road design may be liable if inadequate crosswalks or signals contributed to the crash. Contact Roden Law for a free evaluation.

faqs.question: How quickly should I contact a lawyer after an injury in downtown Charleston?
faqs.answer: As soon as possible. In a tourist-heavy area like Ansonborough, witnesses may be visitors who leave quickly, and surveillance footage may be overwritten within days. While South Carolina allows 3 years to file (S.C. Code § 15-3-530), early attorney involvement helps preserve critical evidence.

Coming Street's narrow lanes and heavy cyclist presence generate frequent bike-vehicle conflicts. The Spring Street and Rutledge Avenue intersection is a complex junction handling traffic from multiple directions including MUSC-bound commuters.

Cannonborough-Elliotborough is one of Charleston's most rapidly evolving neighborhoods, with the Spring Street corridor transforming into a vibrant restaurant and retail destination. This growth has brought increased vehicle, pedestrian, and bicycle traffic to streets originally designed for a quieter residential neighborhood.
Roden Law's Charleston office handles personal injury claims for Cannonborough-Elliotborough residents, including car accidents on Spring Street and Cannon Street, bicycle crashes on Coming Street, and premises liability incidents at the neighborhood's growing number of commercial establishments. Our attorneys understand how rapidly developing neighborhoods create new traffic hazards.
Injured in Cannonborough-Elliotborough? Contact Roden Law for a free case review.

faqs.question: Are bicycle accidents increasing in Cannonborough-Elliotborough?
faqs.answer: Yes. The neighborhood's growing popularity with young professionals who cycle to work and to Upper King Street businesses has increased bike traffic on streets without protected bike infrastructure. Coming Street and Cannon Street are particular hotspots for bike-vehicle conflicts.

faqs.question: Can I file a claim for a construction-related injury in the neighborhood?
faqs.answer: Yes. The neighborhood's rapid development means active construction sites throughout the area. If you're injured due to falling debris, sidewalk obstructions, unsafe scaffolding, or construction vehicle traffic, the contractor, developer, or property owner may be liable.

faqs.question: What if a parked car door opens into my bicycle on Cannon Street?
faqs.answer: This is called 'dooring' and the person who opened the door is liable for your injuries under South Carolina law. Drivers and passengers must check for cyclists before opening doors. These accidents can cause severe injuries and you have the right to full compensation.

name: The Crosstown
h1: Personal Injury Lawyer Serving The Crosstown, Charleston
meta_title: The Crosstown Personal Injury Lawyer | Charleston | Roden Law
meta_description: Injured near The Crosstown (US-17), Charleston? Roden Law handles high-speed car accidents, pedestrian crashes, and truck collisions. Free consultation.
roads: The Crosstown (US-17/Septima Clark Parkway) is a multi-lane divided highway cutting through the Charleston peninsula that generates severe high-speed collisions including head-on crashes in crossover accidents. The Crosstown at Hagood Avenue sees dangerous merging from vehicles entering and exiting the Citadel Mall area and the Joe Riley Stadium complex. The Lockwood Drive and Crosstown interchange creates a complex multi-lane junction with frequent confusion-related accidents. Pedestrian crossings of the Crosstown are extremely dangerous due to wide lanes, high speeds, and limited crossing infrastructure dividing neighborhoods on either side.
hospitals:
MUSC Health — 171 Ashley Ave, Charleston, SC 29425 — (843) 792-2300
Roper Hospital — 316 Calhoun St, Charleston, SC 29401 — (843) 724-2000
landmarks: The Crosstown (US-17/Septima Clark Parkway), Joe Riley Stadium, The Citadel campus (nearby), Brittlebank Park, Lockwood Drive marina district, Charleston City Marina
service_area: Serving The Crosstown corridor, Wagener Terrace, Byrnes Downs, and surrounding Charleston peninsula neighborhoods.
content_hook:
The Crosstown — formally Septima Clark Parkway and part of US-17 — is the multi-lane highway bisecting the lower Charleston peninsula. This high-speed road cutting through dense residential neighborhoods creates one of the city's most dangerous corridors for both vehicle occupants and pedestrians attempting to cross between communities.
Roden Law's Charleston office handles personal injury claims arising from Crosstown accidents, including high-speed vehicle collisions, pedestrian crashes, truck accidents, and incidents at the Lockwood Drive interchange. Our attorneys understand the severity of injuries from highway-speed collisions in an urban environment and fight for maximum compensation.
Injured on The Crosstown? Contact Roden Law for a free case review.

faqs.question: Why are Crosstown accidents so severe?
faqs.answer: The Crosstown carries highway-speed traffic (45-55 mph) through a densely populated urban area. This combination of high speeds and frequent cross-traffic produces severe T-bone, head-on, and pedestrian collisions that result in catastrophic injuries at rates far higher than typical city streets.

faqs.question: Can I file a claim if road design on The Crosstown caused my accident?
faqs.answer: Yes. If inadequate road design — such as missing pedestrian crossings, confusing lane markings, or insufficient lighting — contributed to your accident, the government entity responsible (SCDOT or the City of Charleston) may be liable under the South Carolina Tort Claims Act.

faqs.question: What types of injuries are common in Crosstown accidents?
faqs.answer: Due to the high speeds involved, Crosstown accidents frequently cause traumatic brain injuries, spinal cord injuries, multiple fractures, and fatalities. These severe injuries require experienced legal representation to ensure full compensation for long-term medical needs and life impacts.

meta_description: Injured in Wagener Terrace, Charleston? Roden Law handles car accidents on Rutledge Ave, dog bite claims, and pedestrian injuries. Free consultation.
roads: Rutledge Avenue through Wagener Terrace carries heavy north-south traffic and serves as the primary route between downtown and the Upper Peninsula, with speed and volume creating hazards for the neighborhood's family-oriented pedestrian traffic. The Rutledge Avenue and Grove Street intersection is a blind corner where accidents frequently involve cyclists and pedestrians heading to Hampton Park. King Street Extension through the neighborhood's western edge generates commercial vehicle and commuter traffic adjacent to residential streets. The neighborhood's grid of narrow residential streets sees cut-through traffic from drivers avoiding Rutledge Avenue congestion.
hospitals:
MUSC Health — 171 Ashley Ave, Charleston, SC 29425 — (843) 792-2300

content_hook:
Wagener Terrace is a family-friendly residential neighborhood on Charleston's Upper Peninsula, centered around Hampton Park and adjacent to The Citadel campus. The neighborhood's walkable character, popular dog park, and proximity to Hampton Park make it one of Charleston's most active pedestrian neighborhoods — but its streets also carry increasing commuter traffic.
Roden Law's Charleston office represents Wagener Terrace residents injured in car accidents on Rutledge Avenue, pedestrian and cyclist collisions near Hampton Park, dog bite incidents at the neighborhood's popular off-leash areas, and premises liability cases. Our attorneys are familiar with this neighborhood's unique character and the safety challenges its residents face.
Injured in Wagener Terrace? Contact Roden Law for a free case review.

faqs.question: Can I file a claim for a dog bite injury in Wagener Terrace?
faqs.answer: Yes. Under South Carolina law (S.C. Code § 47-3-110), dog owners are strictly liable for injuries caused by their dogs. This applies whether the bite occurs at Hampton Park, on neighborhood streets, or on private property. You can recover medical expenses, scarring treatment, and pain and suffering.

faqs.question: What if I'm hit by a car while jogging near Hampton Park?
faqs.answer: Pedestrians and joggers injured by negligent drivers can pursue full compensation. The heavy foot traffic around Hampton Park means drivers have a heightened duty of care. Document the scene, get medical attention, and contact Roden Law for a free case evaluation.

faqs.question: Are Citadel cadets liable for off-campus accidents in Wagener Terrace?
faqs.answer: Yes. Citadel cadets are personally liable for negligent driving like any other motorist. If a cadet's negligence caused your accident, you can file a claim against them and their auto insurance. The Citadel itself may have additional liability in certain circumstances.
